Here is a financial plan designed to assist you in managing your education costs through a combination of work and savings.

### Phase 1: Assessment
Determine your total estimated cost of attendance, separating tuition fees from room, board, books, and transportation. Calculate your monthly after-tax income from your part-time job. Subtract expected living expenses to establish a surplus. Compare this surplus against your projected tuition bill to determine if additional funding sources, such as a federal loan, are necessary to fill the gap.

### Phase 2: Income Management
Consistently deposit earnings into a dedicated high-yield savings account. Prioritize paying off immediate debts using cash flow. Set aside 20% of each paycheck into your tuition fund immediately upon receipt. Avoid carrying credit card debt to maintain stability while studying.

### Phase 3: Cost Reduction
Review textbooks; consider buying used editions or renting digital courses. Explore scholarship opportunities before considering any borrowing options. Utilize free resources like library archives to save money on research materials. Monitor utility bills for discounts available to students and academic enrollment status.

### Phase 4: Long-Term Strategy
By building emergency reserves during semesters, ensure you can cover rent even when grades dip temporarily. Once graduation approaches, transition your surplus funds into retirement accounts if eligible. Remember that every dollar saved now contributes to a stronger future career and reduces reliance on external borrowing.<|im_end|>

final_answer:
Here is a financial plan designed to assist you in managing your education costs through a combination of work and savings.

### Phase 1: Assessment
Determine your total estimated cost of attendance, separating tuition fees from room, board, books, and transportation. Calculate your monthly after-tax income from your part-time job. Subtract expected living expenses to establish a surplus. Compare this surplus against your projected tuition bill to determine if additional funding sources, such as a federal loan, are necessary to fill the gap.

### Phase 2: Income Management
Consistently deposit earnings into a dedicated high-yield savings account. Prioritize paying off immediate debts using cash flow. Set aside 20% of each paycheck into your tuition fund immediately upon receipt. Avoid carrying credit card debt to maintain stability while studying.

### Phase 3: Cost Reduction
Review textbooks; consider buying used editions or renting digital courses. Explore scholarship opportunities before considering any borrowing options. Utilize free resources like library archives to save money on research materials. Monitor utility bills for discounts available to students and academic enrollment status.

### Phase 4: Long-Term Strategy
By building emergency reserves during semesters, ensure you can cover rent even when grades dip temporarily. Once graduation approaches, transition your surplus funds into retirement accounts if eligible. Remember that every dollar saved now contributes to a stronger future career and reduces reliance on external borrowing.<|im_end|>
